_comment: Season-average CASH price outlook ($/bu) by marketing year, from public sources. USDA WASDE is the official season-average forecast and the numeric anchor; the analysts we follow (StoneX/Suderman, ISU/Chad Hart, Pro Farmer) mostly comment on it rather than publishing their own clean number, so their cells stay null and they contribute a directional lean + commentary. Populate a source's corn/soybeans values if it publicly states a distinct figure; the dashboard averages all non-null values into the consensus. Edit as new forecasts print.
usda_summary: First 2026/27 print (May WASDE): new-crop soybeans jump to $11.40 — up $1.00 on the year and the highest since 2023/24 — on strong crush and renewable-diesel demand. Corn edges to $4.40 as usage outruns production and pulls ending stocks down. Both still sit near or below most Iowa break-evens (~$5 corn, ~$12 soy), so it's a firmer-but-still-tight picture, not a profit year.
bottom_line: The four line up in a similar place: prices are firmer than a year ago but still near break-even, with corn the steadier of the two and soybeans a China/weather wildcard. The shared message is discipline — price planned tranches into rallies rather than waiting for a runaway move.

sources.name: USDA WASDE
sources.cite: May 2026 WASDE (via Southern Ag Today / Farm Bureau)
sources.note: Official season-average farm price. 2026/27 is the first new-crop estimate: soybeans $11.40 (+$1.00) on renewable-diesel-led crush demand; corn $4.40 as demand outpaces the crop and draws stocks lower.

sources.name: StoneX — Arlan Suderman
sources.note: A big 2026 crop and supportive weather have funds liquidating and the trade focused on supply, not scarcity — near-term pressure. But old-crop Grain Stocks keep printing below expectations; if that holds, USDA may cut production, which is supportive. Soybeans hinge on China: crushers favor cheaper South American beans, so any large U.S. purchase would be a trade-politics decision, not economics.

sources.name: ISU Extension — Chad Hart
sources.note: Margins are the story: 2026 season-average prices still run below Iowa production costs, so the one-year profit outlook is 'troublesome.' Corn is the relative bright spot — usage is outrunning production (2026 looks much like 2024), pulling stocks down and lifting corn modestly, while acres rotate from corn toward soybeans on weak corn economics.

sources.name: Pro Farmer
sources.note: Marketing stance is 'reward rallies' — forward-contract in disciplined clips against set targets and be ready for volatility, especially in soybeans. A bit more optimistic on corn, where old-crop record production is being met by record demand; pegs new-crop soybean season-average near $11.40.
